title: Explain User Spaces
short_answer: User Spaces are manual memory areas controlled by the user.
text: User Spaces are for information the user intentionally saves and organizes. They should stay separate from inferred machine memory and separate from the HELP manual.

title: Explain Manual Memory
short_answer: Manual memory is information saved on purpose by the user.
text: Manual memory is different from inferred memory. It includes saved notes, rules, prompts, projects, and other user-controlled information.

title: Explain Editable Memory
short_answer: Editable memory is memory the user can add, change, or remove.
text: Editable memory should be user-controlled. It is useful for saved facts, special instructions, reusable prompts, and grouped project information.

title: Explain Notes
short_answer: Notes are saved facts, reminders, ideas, or loose information.
text: Use notes when the information is useful later but is not a rule, prompt, or project. Notes are the simplest manual memory bucket.

title: Explain Rules
short_answer: Rules are saved behavior instructions for the assistant.
text: Rules are not random facts. They tell the assistant how to behave, what to avoid, what tone to use, or what response pattern to follow.

title: Explain Prompts
short_answer: Prompts are saved instruction patterns the user may reuse.
text: Prompts in User Spaces are reusable instruction templates. They are different from internal LLM prompts, but both shape how the assistant answers.

title: Explain Projects
short_answer: Projects are grouped manual memory items for a named effort.
text: Use projects when saved information belongs to a specific workstream, app, idea, build, or long-running topic.

title: Explain Collections
short_answer: Collections are grouped saved items under one theme.
text: Collections help avoid one giant pile of saved memory. They let the user group related information, like recipes, project notes, rule groups, or reference lists.

title: Explain Saved Data
short_answer: Saved Data is information intentionally stored so it can be found later.
text: Saved Data can include facts, notes, reminders, project info, rule sets, prompts, or other useful manual knowledge.

title: Explain Custom Data
short_answer: Custom Data is user-defined saved information that may not fit a default bucket.
text: Custom Data matters because the user may want to save unusual but useful information, like a book outline, personal system map, collection, or special reference list.
